Software testing tools supporting a DevOps approach.
Automated Testing for Enterprise Applications

DevOps Test Hub delivers enterprise grade software testing to support organisations during their DevOps transformations and cloud native modernisations. To achieve reliable DevOps deliveries, teams must automate their testing to reduce the risk of deploying poor quality software into production.

DevOps Test Hub is a cloud native continuous testing platform for small and large teams, it offers a collaborative user experience, bringing together different teams and disciplines of test automation in an enterprise grade, secure and scalable platform.

Capabilities
UI Testing: Code free automation for web, mobile and desktop, as well as legacy systems like Mainframes, SAP and Oracle.
API Testing: Trigger and validate a system’s APIs and Interfaces across a range of network protocols and message formats.
Performance Testing: Validate the scalability of enterprise applications, identify the presence and cause of system performance bottlenecks, track performance changes between versions.
Service Virtualization: Accelerate and enhance test cycles by using Virtual Services to stand in for missing or undesirable system dependencies.
Test Data Fabrication: Generate synthetic data without the risk of data leaks or privacy concern, all on demand.

To see DevOps Test Hub in action click the image at the top of this page. For more detailed information, click here to view the latest DevOps Test product documentation.

Tutorial Overview

Enterprise applications come in many different shapes and sizes - for this tutorial we will work with 'Emerald', an eCommerce furniture store. Emerald is HCL Commerce Cloud's reference implementation of a B2C store front and for our purposes represents the system under test. We will test the Emerald application using DevOps Test, exploring some of the different dimensions of test automation and mitigating various common software delivery risks. Pre-created API, UI and Performance tests are provided for simplicity.

Get started by clicking the 'LAUNCH IT!' button, and read on for detailed steps on how to navigate the tester experience.

Pre-requisites
GIT account (e.g., github.com) into which you will fork the provided test repositories. This is to ensure you have full read/write capabilities to the tests, and to avoid multi-user conflicts.
Approximately 60 minutes to walk through the tutorial.
[Optional] An installation of the DevOps Test workbench products on your own machine.
Test Assets

In keeping with modern development practices, our test assets are stored in GIT repositories where they are branched, versioned and easily shareable. The tests we will execute against Emerald are available in GIT using the below URLs, these repositories must be forked into your own GIT account before proceeding with the tutorial. Follow this link for more details on forking repositories with GitHub.

There are several branches in the provided GIT repositories, and for the purposes of this tutorial we will always use DEV, since this relates the set of test assets to the version of Emerald we have deployed in our Marketplace development sandbox. Using one of the other branches may result in test execution failures since the test assets are designed to run against older, or newer versions of Emerald in different environments like UAT or PRE-PROD.

Repository Type	URL
DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, DevOps Test UI & DevOps Test Performance Tests	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests.git
OpenSource Postman & JMeter Scripts	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-Other-Tests.git
System Model	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-System-Model.git
Coverage of Test Suites

Terminology in the testing domain is difficult to pin down, so for the sake of this document, and from the perspective of our DevOps Test automation scripts, we will define Tests and Test Suites. Typically, in this demo we will be executing Test Suites, since that is where the majority of auditable test execution happens. Users can of course still run individual tests - these can be seen in DevOps Test Hub separately from Suites

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min
Guided Tutorial

The following sections of this document describe steps to prepare our DevOps Test Hub with the provided test repositories, before executing and analysing their results

Step 1 - Solution Readiness
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.





Curl Samples
Videos

The provided videos offer some guidance on the above tutorials. Note that there are small differences between the video and tutorial steps. There are also minor differences between the version of DevOps Test Hub used in the video and the one in your sandbox, users should refer to the written tutorial which is correct as at the current release and use the video for general guidance only.

Software testing tools supporting a DevOps approach.
Automated Testing for Enterprise Applications

DevOps Test Hub delivers enterprise grade software testing to support organisations during their DevOps transformations and cloud native modernisations. To achieve reliable DevOps deliveries, teams must automate their testing to reduce the risk of deploying poor quality software into production.

DevOps Test Hub is a cloud native continuous testing platform for small and large teams, it offers a collaborative user experience, bringing together different teams and disciplines of test automation in an enterprise grade, secure and scalable platform.

Capabilities
UI Testing: Code free automation for web, mobile and desktop, as well as legacy systems like Mainframes, SAP and Oracle.
API Testing: Trigger and validate a system’s APIs and Interfaces across a range of network protocols and message formats.
Performance Testing: Validate the scalability of enterprise applications, identify the presence and cause of system performance bottlenecks, track performance changes between versions.
Service Virtualization: Accelerate and enhance test cycles by using Virtual Services to stand in for missing or undesirable system dependencies.
Test Data Fabrication: Generate synthetic data without the risk of data leaks or privacy concern, all on demand.

To see DevOps Test Hub in action click the image at the top of this page. For more detailed information, click here to view the latest DevOps Test product documentation.

Tutorial Overview

Enterprise applications come in many different shapes and sizes - for this tutorial we will work with 'Emerald', an eCommerce furniture store. Emerald is HCL Commerce Cloud's reference implementation of a B2C store front and for our purposes represents the system under test. We will test the Emerald application using DevOps Test, exploring some of the different dimensions of test automation and mitigating various common software delivery risks. Pre-created API, UI and Performance tests are provided for simplicity.

Get started by clicking the 'LAUNCH IT!' button, and read on for detailed steps on how to navigate the tester experience.

Pre-requisites
GIT account (e.g., github.com) into which you will fork the provided test repositories. This is to ensure you have full read/write capabilities to the tests, and to avoid multi-user conflicts.
Approximately 60 minutes to walk through the tutorial.
[Optional] An installation of the DevOps Test workbench products on your own machine.
Test Assets

In keeping with modern development practices, our test assets are stored in GIT repositories where they are branched, versioned and easily shareable. The tests we will execute against Emerald are available in GIT using the below URLs, these repositories must be forked into your own GIT account before proceeding with the tutorial. Follow this link for more details on forking repositories with GitHub.

There are several branches in the provided GIT repositories, and for the purposes of this tutorial we will always use DEV, since this relates the set of test assets to the version of Emerald we have deployed in our Marketplace development sandbox. Using one of the other branches may result in test execution failures since the test assets are designed to run against older, or newer versions of Emerald in different environments like UAT or PRE-PROD.

Repository Type	URL
DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, DevOps Test UI & DevOps Test Performance Tests	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests.git
OpenSource Postman & JMeter Scripts	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-Other-Tests.git
System Model	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-System-Model.git
Coverage of Test Suites

Terminology in the testing domain is difficult to pin down, so for the sake of this document, and from the perspective of our DevOps Test automation scripts, we will define Tests and Test Suites. Typically, in this demo we will be executing Test Suites, since that is where the majority of auditable test execution happens. Users can of course still run individual tests - these can be seen in DevOps Test Hub separately from Suites

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min

Step 1 - Solution Readiness
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.

Automated Testing for Enterprise Applications

DevOps Test Hub delivers enterprise grade software testing to support organisations during their DevOps transformations and cloud native modernisations. To achieve reliable DevOps deliveries, teams must automate their testing to reduce the risk of deploying poor quality software into production.

DevOps Test Hub is a cloud native continuous testing platform for small and large teams, it offers a collaborative user experience, bringing together different teams and disciplines of test automation in an enterprise grade, secure and scalable platform.

Capabilities
UI Testing: Code free automation for web, mobile and desktop, as well as legacy systems like Mainframes, SAP and Oracle.
API Testing: Trigger and validate a system’s APIs and Interfaces across a range of network protocols and message formats.
Performance Testing: Validate the scalability of enterprise applications, identify the presence and cause of system performance bottlenecks, track performance changes between versions.
Service Virtualization: Accelerate and enhance test cycles by using Virtual Services to stand in for missing or undesirable system dependencies.
Test Data Fabrication: Generate synthetic data without the risk of data leaks or privacy concern, all on demand.

To see DevOps Test Hub in action click the image at the top of this page. For more detailed information, click here to view the latest DevOps Test product documentation.

Tutorial Overview

Enterprise applications come in many different shapes and sizes - for this tutorial we will work with 'Emerald', an eCommerce furniture store. Emerald is HCL Commerce Cloud's reference implementation of a B2C store front and for our purposes represents the system under test. We will test the Emerald application using DevOps Test, exploring some of the different dimensions of test automation and mitigating various common software delivery risks. Pre-created API, UI and Performance tests are provided for simplicity.

Get started by clicking the 'LAUNCH IT!' button, and read on for detailed steps on how to navigate the tester experience.

Pre-requisites
GIT account (e.g., github.com) into which you will fork the provided test repositories. This is to ensure you have full read/write capabilities to the tests, and to avoid multi-user conflicts.
Approximately 60 minutes to walk through the tutorial.
[Optional] An installation of the DevOps Test workbench products on your own machine.
Test Assets

In keeping with modern development practices, our test assets are stored in GIT repositories where they are branched, versioned and easily shareable. The tests we will execute against Emerald are available in GIT using the below URLs, these repositories must be forked into your own GIT account before proceeding with the tutorial. Follow this link for more details on forking repositories with GitHub.

There are several branches in the provided GIT repositories, and for the purposes of this tutorial we will always use DEV, since this relates the set of test assets to the version of Emerald we have deployed in our Marketplace development sandbox. Using one of the other branches may result in test execution failures since the test assets are designed to run against older, or newer versions of Emerald in different environments like UAT or PRE-PROD.

Repository Type	URL
DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, DevOps Test UI & DevOps Test Performance Tests	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests.git
OpenSource Postman & JMeter Scripts	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-Other-Tests.git
System Model	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-System-Model.git
Coverage of Test Suites

Terminology in the testing domain is difficult to pin down, so for the sake of this document, and from the perspective of our DevOps Test automation scripts, we will define Tests and Test Suites. Typically, in this demo we will be executing Test Suites, since that is where the majority of auditable test execution happens. Users can of course still run individual tests - these can be seen in DevOps Test Hub separately from Suites

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.

Emerald is HCL Commerce Cloud's reference implementation of a B2C store front and for our purposes represents the system under test. We will test the Emerald application using DevOps Test, exploring some of the different dimensions of test automation and mitigating various common software delivery risks. Pre-created API, UI and Performance tests are provided for simplicity.

Get started by clicking the 'LAUNCH IT!' button, and read on for detailed steps on how to navigate the tester experience.

Pre-requisites
GIT account (e.g., github.com) into which you will fork the provided test repositories. This is to ensure you have full read/write capabilities to the tests, and to avoid multi-user conflicts.
Approximately 60 minutes to walk through the tutorial.
[Optional] An installation of the DevOps Test workbench products on your own machine.
Test Assets

In keeping with modern development practices, our test assets are stored in GIT repositories where they are branched, versioned and easily shareable. The tests we will execute against Emerald are available in GIT using the below URLs, these repositories must be forked into your own GIT account before proceeding with the tutorial. Follow this link for more details on forking repositories with GitHub.

There are several branches in the provided GIT repositories, and for the purposes of this tutorial we will always use DEV, since this relates the set of test assets to the version of Emerald we have deployed in our Marketplace development sandbox. Using one of the other branches may result in test execution failures since the test assets are designed to run against older, or newer versions of Emerald in different environments like UAT or PRE-PROD.

Repository Type	URL
DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, DevOps Test UI & DevOps Test Performance Tests	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests.git
OpenSource Postman & JMeter Scripts	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-Other-Tests.git
System Model	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-System-Model.git
Coverage of Test Suites

Terminology in the testing domain is difficult to pin down, so for the sake of this document, and from the perspective of our DevOps Test automation scripts, we will define Tests and Test Suites. Typically, in this demo we will be executing Test Suites, since that is where the majority of auditable test execution happens. Users can of course still run individual tests - these can be seen in DevOps Test Hub separately from Suites

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.

Automated Testing for Enterprise Applications

DevOps Test Hub delivers enterprise grade software testing to support organisations during their DevOps transformations and cloud native modernisations. To achieve reliable DevOps deliveries, teams must automate their testing to reduce the risk of deploying poor quality software into production.

DevOps Test Hub is a cloud native continuous testing platform for small and large teams, it offers a collaborative user experience, bringing together different teams and disciplines of test automation in an enterprise grade, secure and scalable platform.

Capabilities
UI Testing: Code free automation for web, mobile and desktop, as well as legacy systems like Mainframes, SAP and Oracle.
API Testing: Trigger and validate a system’s APIs and Interfaces across a range of network protocols and message formats.
Performance Testing: Validate the scalability of enterprise applications, identify the presence and cause of system performance bottlenecks, track performance changes between versions.
Service Virtualization: Accelerate and enhance test cycles by using Virtual Services to stand in for missing or undesirable system dependencies.
Test Data Fabrication: Generate synthetic data without the risk of data leaks or privacy concern, all on demand.

To see DevOps Test Hub in action click the image at the top of this page. For more detailed information, click here to view the latest DevOps Test product documentation.

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min
Guided Tutorial

The following sections of this document describe steps to prepare our DevOps Test Hub with the provided test repositories, before executing and analysing their results

Step 1 - Solution Readiness
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.

There are also minor differences between the version of DevOps Test Hub used in the video and the one in your sandbox, users should refer to the written tutorial which is correct as at the current release and use the video for general guidance only.

Software testing tools supporting a DevOps approach.
Automated Testing for Enterprise Applications

DevOps Test Hub delivers enterprise grade software testing to support organisations during their DevOps transformations and cloud native modernisations. To achieve reliable DevOps deliveries, teams must automate their testing to reduce the risk of deploying poor quality software into production.

DevOps Test Hub is a cloud native continuous testing platform for small and large teams, it offers a collaborative user experience, bringing together different teams and disciplines of test automation in an enterprise grade, secure and scalable platform.

Capabilities
UI Testing: Code free automation for web, mobile and desktop, as well as legacy systems like Mainframes, SAP and Oracle.
API Testing: Trigger and validate a system’s APIs and Interfaces across a range of network protocols and message formats.
Performance Testing: Validate the scalability of enterprise applications, identify the presence and cause of system performance bottlenecks, track performance changes between versions.
Service Virtualization: Accelerate and enhance test cycles by using Virtual Services to stand in for missing or undesirable system dependencies.
Test Data Fabrication: Generate synthetic data without the risk of data leaks or privacy concern, all on demand.

To see DevOps Test Hub in action click the image at the top of this page. For more detailed information, click here to view the latest DevOps Test product documentation.

Tutorial Overview

Enterprise applications come in many different shapes and sizes - for this tutorial we will work with 'Emerald', an eCommerce furniture store. Emerald is HCL Commerce Cloud's reference implementation of a B2C store front and for our purposes represents the system under test. We will test the Emerald application using DevOps Test, exploring some of the different dimensions of test automation and mitigating various common software delivery risks. Pre-created API, UI and Performance tests are provided for simplicity.

Get started by clicking the 'LAUNCH IT!' button, and read on for detailed steps on how to navigate the tester experience.

Pre-requisites
GIT account (e.g., github.com) into which you will fork the provided test repositories. This is to ensure you have full read/write capabilities to the tests, and to avoid multi-user conflicts.
Approximately 60 minutes to walk through the tutorial.
[Optional] An installation of the DevOps Test workbench products on your own machine.
Test Assets

In keeping with modern development practices, our test assets are stored in GIT repositories where they are branched, versioned and easily shareable. The tests we will execute against Emerald are available in GIT using the below URLs, these repositories must be forked into your own GIT account before proceeding with the tutorial. Follow this link for more details on forking repositories with GitHub.

There are several branches in the provided GIT repositories, and for the purposes of this tutorial we will always use DEV, since this relates the set of test assets to the version of Emerald we have deployed in our Marketplace development sandbox. Using one of the other branches may result in test execution failures since the test assets are designed to run against older, or newer versions of Emerald in different environments like UAT or PRE-PROD.

Repository Type	URL
DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, DevOps Test UI & DevOps Test Performance Tests	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests.git
OpenSource Postman & JMeter Scripts	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-Other-Tests.git
System Model	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-System-Model.git
Coverage of Test Suites

Terminology in the testing domain is difficult to pin down, so for the sake of this document, and from the perspective of our DevOps Test automation scripts, we will define Tests and Test Suites. Typically, in this demo we will be executing Test Suites, since that is where the majority of auditable test execution happens. Users can of course still run individual tests - these can be seen in DevOps Test Hub separately from Suites

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min
Guided Tutorial

The following sections of this document describe steps to prepare our DevOps Test Hub with the provided test repositories, before executing and analysing their results

Step 1 - Solution Readiness
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.





Curl Samples
Videos

The provided videos offer some guidance on the above tutorials. Note that there are small differences between the video and tutorial steps. There are also minor differences between the version of DevOps Test Hub used in the video and the one in your sandbox, users should refer to the written tutorial which is correct as at the current release and use the video for general guidance only.

There are also minor differences between the version of DevOps Test Hub used in the video and the one in your sandbox, users should refer to the written tutorial which is correct as at the current release and use the video for general guidance only.

Products Involved

Software testing tools supporting a DevOps approach.
Automated Testing for Enterprise Applications

DevOps Test Hub delivers enterprise grade software testing to support organisations during their DevOps transformations and cloud native modernisations. To achieve reliable DevOps deliveries, teams must automate their testing to reduce the risk of deploying poor quality software into production.

DevOps Test Hub is a cloud native continuous testing platform for small and large teams, it offers a collaborative user experience, bringing together different teams and disciplines of test automation in an enterprise grade, secure and scalable platform.

Capabilities
UI Testing: Code free automation for web, mobile and desktop, as well as legacy systems like Mainframes, SAP and Oracle.
API Testing: Trigger and validate a system’s APIs and Interfaces across a range of network protocols and message formats.
Performance Testing: Validate the scalability of enterprise applications, identify the presence and cause of system performance bottlenecks, track performance changes between versions.
Service Virtualization: Accelerate and enhance test cycles by using Virtual Services to stand in for missing or undesirable system dependencies.
Test Data Fabrication: Generate synthetic data without the risk of data leaks or privacy concern, all on demand.

To see DevOps Test Hub in action click the image at the top of this page. For more detailed information, click here to view the latest DevOps Test product documentation.

Tutorial Overview

Enterprise applications come in many different shapes and sizes - for this tutorial we will work with 'Emerald', an eCommerce furniture store. Emerald is HCL Commerce Cloud's reference implementation of a B2C store front and for our purposes represents the system under test. We will test the Emerald application using DevOps Test, exploring some of the different dimensions of test automation and mitigating various common software delivery risks. Pre-created API, UI and Performance tests are provided for simplicity.

Get started by clicking the 'LAUNCH IT!' button, and read on for detailed steps on how to navigate the tester experience.

Pre-requisites
GIT account (e.g., github.com) into which you will fork the provided test repositories. This is to ensure you have full read/write capabilities to the tests, and to avoid multi-user conflicts.
Approximately 60 minutes to walk through the tutorial.
[Optional] An installation of the DevOps Test workbench products on your own machine.
Test Assets

In keeping with modern development practices, our test assets are stored in GIT repositories where they are branched, versioned and easily shareable. The tests we will execute against Emerald are available in GIT using the below URLs, these repositories must be forked into your own GIT account before proceeding with the tutorial. Follow this link for more details on forking repositories with GitHub.

There are several branches in the provided GIT repositories, and for the purposes of this tutorial we will always use DEV, since this relates the set of test assets to the version of Emerald we have deployed in our Marketplace development sandbox. Using one of the other branches may result in test execution failures since the test assets are designed to run against older, or newer versions of Emerald in different environments like UAT or PRE-PROD.

Repository Type	URL
DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, DevOps Test UI & DevOps Test Performance Tests	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests.git
OpenSource Postman & JMeter Scripts	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-Other-Tests.git
System Model	https://github.com/HCL-TECH-SOFTWARE/Emerald-System-Model.git
Coverage of Test Suites

Terminology in the testing domain is difficult to pin down, so for the sake of this document, and from the perspective of our DevOps Test automation scripts, we will define Tests and Test Suites. Typically, in this demo we will be executing Test Suites, since that is where the majority of auditable test execution happens. Users can of course still run individual tests - these can be seen in DevOps Test Hub separately from Suites

Tests: A unit of automation that may be a complete script to test against a given verdict, or which may be a smaller modular unit that can be used to construct a larger testable scenario.
Test Suite: Test Suites orchestrate Tests Scripts in sequence or in parallel and can be made up of modular tests that combine to perform a given test function.
Compound Test: A type of Test Suite which is often used to bring together modular tests that span different disciplines of test, with one auditable verdict and report, often used for end to end testing. For example a Compound Test may contain a browser test, followed by a mobile test, an API test, a check in a database, and a test running against a desktop or thick client application. Data and variables are shared from one test type to the next in order to validate a complete end to end business process.

Type	Test Suite Name	Details	Avg Execution Duration
API	Add To Cart	API Suite (8 tests) adding products and validating shopping cart	<1 min
API	Add To Cart and Checkout	API Suite (16 tests) including checkout and payment processing	<1 min
User Interface	Register Users and Add Address	Registration of news user(s) in the Emerald application and additional of a default shipping address. Driven by Users.csv dataset or 'Users' Data Fabrication schema	~5 min
User Interface	Login Add Item and Checkout	Compound Tests comprising 3 UI tests to login, add an item into the shopping basket and to complete the entire checkout and payment process. [Pre-requisite - Users must have been created first by running the Register Users and Add Address test]	~4 min
Performance	BenchmarkB2CFlow	VU Schedule comprising 3 transactions (11 tests) for Guest User browse, search and checkout flows	~5 min
JMeter	Emerald_Homepage	Simple load test, invoking Homepage, Dining, Kitchen, Bath, Living and Bedroom pages	<1 min
Postman	Personalized Shopping Experience	Comprising 4 API calls without test assertions including Market Spot Data, Shopper Session and Add Qualifying Items to Cart	<1 min
Guided Tutorial

The following sections of this document describe steps to prepare our DevOps Test Hub with the provided test repositories, before executing and analysing their results

Step 1 - Solution Readiness
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure

Integrations & APIs

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.

Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.

Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.

There are also minor differences between the version of DevOps Test Hub used in the video and the one in your sandbox, users should refer to the written tutorial which is correct as at the current release and use the video for general guidance only.

The following sections of this document describe steps to prepare our DevOps Test Hub with the provided test repositories, before executing and analysing their results

If you have not done so already, start the business solution by clicking the 'LAUNCH' button from the HCLSoftware Marketplace Business Solutions catalog.  When the View Sandbox Admin Console button activates, login using the provided username/password and monitor the solution content tiles to see whether our DevOps Test Hub and Emerald applications are ready. The DevOps Test Hub tile should activate very quickly and will indicate readiness with a green tick icon. Emerald can take between 10 and 30 minutes to become fully ready, but we can start to prepare the DevOps Test Hub while we wait.
A link to launch the DevOps Test Hub dashboard is available by clicking 'General Information' - Click the 'Open Link' button for the DevOps Test Hub homepage.

We will use Prometheus to monitor our Emerald kubernetes node for environmental metrics collection and to support our analysis of performance test results. While we are in the Sandbox Admin Console we can take a note of the Prometheus IP address which we will use later in the tutorial.

In the Sandbox Admin Console, navigate to the to the Kubernetes Resources page and click on the 'Pods' widget. Select 'Services' at the top of the page and search for 'prometheus' - one service should be listed which displays the Cluster IP value and Port. Take note of the IP/Port for later use.
Take note of the IP/Port for later use

When the Emerald application tile indicates it is ready, click 'General Information' and open the link for the 'Emerald (B2C) Store' which will take us to the Emerald homepage. Take a note of the Emerald hostname which will look something like 'commerce-preview.sbx1234.play.hclsofy.com'. The value sbxNNNN is unique to your sandbox and will be needed once we start to execute tests.
Take a note of the Emerald hostname

Try using Emerald yourself to familiarise yourself by finding products, looking at the product details pages and ultimately placing an order. Hint - the Visa credit card number 4111 1111 1111 1111 generates a faked payment authorization and allows for a completion of an order.
We do not need to wait for Emerald before continuing to Step 2, but we will need Emerald to be fully ready before we start running tests
We do not need to wait for Emerald before continuing to Step 2, but we will need Emerald to be fully ready before we start running tests
We do not need to wait for Emerald before continuing to Step 2, but we will need Emerald to be fully ready before we start running tests
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Step 2 - Configure DevOps Test Hub Project And Tests
Register a user and prepare the test Project
Once the DevOps Test Hub is loaded, you'll need to log in using demo.user provided buy the platform.

When logged in, we land in the 'Initial' Teamspace which we will use during this tutorial. In productive use, it is likely that you will want to partition DevOps Test Hub with multiple Teamspaces, one for each team. A teamspace encapsulates lots of reusable configuration and information such as product licenses, agent registration, resource monitor configuration, cloud credentials and system models.

We will add a system model to our Teamspace by loading the 'Emerald_SystemModel' repository from your GIT account. System models allow our team to share valuable information about the different tiers and components in our environment, as well as the dependencies between them. Testers can use this model to identify areas for prioritisation of test coverage, and to associate and analyse test results in context of the system components they relate to.

Add the system model repository by clicking 'Initial' from the navigation pane and selecting 'Manage team space' and 'Repository' to add a new system model. Paste the URL for the provided Emerald_SystemModel.git repository from your own GitHub account and enter your credentials. Once initialized, the system model representing our Emerald application is visible by clicking the back arrow at the top of the screen to return to our Teamspace, and navigating to 'System Model'. Later in the tutorial we will begin to associate tests with different system components, but for the moment, there isn't much to see in the model.

Next, we need to create a new Project inside our Teamspace named 'Emerald' where we will begin to add the test assets. Click the 'New project' button and give the project a name 'Emerald' and an optional description.

We should now be inside our Emerald project at the project configuration page. Here we can set the visibility of our project, public if we'd like other users to see that our project exists, and private if we want to keep our project out of eyesight. There are other configurations for permissive and restrictive access to teamspaces and projects, but we will leave that for the administrator!

Under our project 'Configuration' tab you'll find the 'Add Repository' link. Ensuring you have alreday forked the provided repos into your own GIT account, add the API, UI, Performance, OpenSource Postman and JMeter repositories. We can import each repository individually by providing the repo URL and credentials one by one, but it is quicker to import multiple repos by entering the provider URL (e.g., https://github.com) and selecting the ones we want from a list. In our case, we want the Emerald test repos and we can filter them using the search bar to add 'Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests' and 'Emerald-Other-Tests'.

Once the repositories are cloned to the DevOps Test Hub we can switch to the 'Overview' section of our project from the left hand navigation menu. Here we see information about our project including the types of tests present in our project, and various metrics that will be blank, since we haven't yet run any tests.
Ensure the correct test assets are being used by selecting the GIT branch 'DEV' - this should be active under the Branch selector at the top of the Overview page.
Ensure the correct test assets are being used by selecting the GIT branch 'DEV' - this should be active under the Branch selector at the top of the Overview page.
Ensure the correct test assets are being used by selecting the GIT branch 'DEV' - this should be active under the Branch selector at the top of the Overview page.
Before running any tests, ensure the DEV branch has been selected to ensure the correct version of test assets are used against the current production DevOps Test Hub and Commerce Cloud Emerald App.
Before running any tests, ensure the DEV branch has been selected to ensure the correct version of test assets are used against the current production DevOps Test Hub and Commerce Cloud Emerald App.
Before running any tests, ensure the DEV branch has been selected to ensure the correct version of test assets are used against the current production DevOps Test Hub and Commerce Cloud Emerald App.
Now we have tests in our project, we can go ahead and look at the many features available to us in the server dashboard.

Start by opening the 'Execute' pane in the left navigation. We see a list of Test Suites which relate to the GIT Branch we've selected. If we change the branch we note that some new tests appear and some disappear, this is because different branches can contain different sets of tests relating to the different versions of our application under test and DevOps Test makes it easy to switch between them, this is especially useful when we trigger tests as a part of a pipeline or an automated release process.

We will start by running one of our UI Test Suites "Register Users and Add Address". Click on the execute button (blue circle with a white triangle) which opens the execution properties dialog. There are lots of things to look at here so don't start it yet!

Version: Within the selected GIT Branch, the user can choose which version of the test asset is to be executed.
Schedule: Run Now, or schedule the test to execute at a later time.
Label: Add a label to help identify the test execution results later.
Environment: Select a target environment to execute the test - Only applicable for tests that have been configured with multiple target environments.
Data Sources: Choose to override any pre configured Data Source by swapping in another, or by selecting a Data Fabrication schema to generate fresh data.
Variables: Initialize the value of test variables, or provide a varInit file with a set of input variables.
Location: Choose to override the execution location for the test - Run the test on a remote target rather than from the OTS kubernetes node.
Resource Monitoring: Select a pre-configured resource monitor label to store metrics from the environment under test along with test results.
Results: Associate a test execution with an external test management solution asset.
Advanced: Provide overrides to the various test engines as required.

The Emerald application we're testing was freshly provisioned when we started this tutorial, and when we wrote our tests we could never predict the hostname it would be given. Our tests need to know where Emerald is, and so we need to add two variables in the execution dialog; 'hostname' and 'port_num' - set the values as per your own Emerald deployment. Once you execute the test for the first time, these variables become 'sticky' and persist so that you won't need to enter them again.

Variable Name
Example Value



hostname
commerce-preview.sbxNNNN.play.hclsofy.com


port_num
443

Next, we can add an appropriate label to the test, something like 'Release 9.1.10 Sprint 1' would be appropriate for a manually triggered test. For API or externally driven test executions, consider labelling with meaningful information that your team can relate to, eg "HCL Launch ID 100047", "Azure Pipeline ID 100001", "Build ID 3524" etc.

Finally, press the 'Execute' button. Our Test Suite is now staged for execution on our Kubernetes node, where a dedicated pod will be created to execute and report back the results. It will take a few minutes for it to finish registering our dummy users in the Emerald system - we will leave it running for now and come back to look at the results later.

There are two suites of integration tests which exercise Emerald's internal and external facing APIs, picking products, adding to a cart and ultimately performing a full checkout process. When we execute the API suites "Add to Cart" and "Add to Cart and Checkout" we need to choose a target environment which for our purposes should be set to 'Commerce React Store PRIVATE'. The PUBLIC target environment expects a public URL in order to access the API endpoints, much like our UI test did. We could use this PUBLIC environment and provide variables to initialise our Emerald hostname, but in this case we use PRIVATE instead which addresses the endpoints using their internal kubernetes service names meaning the tests are fully portable, so long as the tests run inside the same cluster as the Emerald application.
After setting the correct environment and adding a label, we can hit Execute. Do the same for both API suites and they will stage for execution.

We can take a look in the Progress page (Execute...> Progress) to view the currently running and recently finished tests. Tests move through 3 stages 'In Transition', 'Running' and 'Complete', and will present a verdict indicated by a colour; Green for success, Amber for an Error during execution and Red indicating a test failure. Clicking the hamburger (3 vertical dots) opens the list of available logs and consoles for the test, including the kubernetes pod logs, test engine console and a test log.

Open Source test tools are a quick and easy way to get started with basic test automation, they can be used to encourage collaboration between developers (who have limited scope for their testing and who are comfortable writing code) and testers (who are expected to go beyond basic test conditions and who see efficiency from codeless testing methods). With collaboration in mind, DevOps Test embraces Open Source and incorporates assets directly into the platform, allowing users the same version control, execution configuration and reporting framework provided to the DevOps Test native test types. In some cases DevOps Test offers a migration path for Open Source tests, extending the value and capability of those tests without the inefficiency of having to rewrite them.

Postman
JMeter 
JUnit
HCL AppScan Codesweep
We will execute the "Personalized Shopping Experience" Postman collection which contains a similar basic set of API tests as seen earlier for checking out a shopping basket programmatically.

Open the execution properties dialog and select the environment 'HCL Commerce Cloud SoFy Demo 1 Environment'. As with our API tests, the Postman tests have been configured to use Kubernetes services names to address the API endpoints. Unlike our DevOps Test Integrations and APIs tests, Postman needs an extra argument in order to bypass the security warning given when working with Emerald's self signed certificates. Before clicking 'Execute'; in the 'Advanced' settings on the execution properties page, add the argument --insecure in the 'Program Arguments' text box. This argument needs to be set once only and is persisted via sticky settings for subsequent execution.

Now we'll start the JMeter performance test "Emerald Homepage". The JMeter test addresses API endpoints like Postman and DevOps Test Integrations and APIs using internal service names, meaning no special properties are required before we hit 'Execute'.

Performance testing is a critical part of any application lifecycle, sadly though, many organisations act too late in a delivery lifecycle to mitigate serious problems and delays when performance issues are eventually found. DevOps Test Performance is an all purpose performance testing product that supports a true performance engineering approach; allowing organisations to 'shift left' their performance testing by introducing low intensity tests targetted as individual components, often driven through APIs and interfaces like Kafka, JMS, MQ, Sockets etc. Traditional performance testing is still a key, and core function of DevOps Test Performance, and by combining low and high intensity performance tests at different stages of an application delivery lifecycle, the risks inherent in late cycle testing can be mitigated and efforts targeted in the right places for maximum effect. In this tutorial, we primarily look at the more traditional style of HTTP performance test.
The "BenchmarkB2CFlow" performance schedule simulates three different classes of guest user in the Emerald application:
The "BenchmarkB2CFlow" performance schedule simulates three different classes of guest user in the Emerald application:

Guest Browse 54% of users who open the Emerald Homepage and browser to a random category and product detail page.
Guest Search 36% of uers who open the Emerald Homepage, then search for a random product using the search bar.
Guest Checkout 10% of users who select a random product and complete a full checkout and order process.
Guest Browse 54% of users who open the Emerald Homepage and browser to a random category and product detail page.
Guest Search 36% of uers who open the Emerald Homepage, then search for a random product using the search bar.
Guest Checkout 10% of users who select a random product and complete a full checkout and order process.

For simplicity during this tutorial, and because we are working with a small demo sandbox, the scope of this performance schedule is limited to 10 concurrent Virtual Users for 1 minute. In productive use, DevOps Test Performance simulates the desired number of virtual users or transactions/second demanded by the business, this can be into the tens or hundreds of thousands of concurrent actions.

Before starting the "BenchmarkB2CFlow", we will configure a set of resource monitors to collect metrics while our test is running. These metrics help us identify the causes of poor application performance and can provide the evidence required to debug and tune complex applications.

To add a Resource Monitoring configuration, move to the Resource Monitoring page (Infrastructure...>Resource Monitoring) and add a new Prometheus Server monitoring source. Leave the target pointing at our current node and add the IP address and Port (80) that we located in the Sandbox Admin Console earlier (Kubernetes Resources...>Services...>xxx-prometheus-server). Click 'Add' and once a connection is established, we see a list of collectable data queries. Select all, or a range of the default values presented by the prometheus server to capture cpu/memory/network metrics and then save the configuration.
For more complex environments, or for more succinct presentation of specific metrics, we can specify our own queries, for example:

sum(system_cpu_usage{app="HCL-Commerce"}) by (pod) would present system cpu usage for only those pods belonging to HCL-Commerce application.
avg(irate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{pod=~"comdemo.*"}[1m])) by (pod) would present cpu usage for only those pods with 'comdemo' in the name, excluding the DevOps Test Hub running pods. 
sum (container_memory_usage_bytes{pod=~"comdemo.*"}) by (pod) would collect only memory utilized by the comdemo Emerald pods
sum(system_cpu_usage{app="HCL-Commerce"}) by (pod) would present system cpu usage for only those pods belonging to HCL-Commerce application.
avg(irate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{pod=~"comdemo.*"}[1m])) by (pod) would present cpu usage for only those pods with 'comdemo' in the name, excluding the DevOps Test Hub running pods.
sum (container_memory_usage_bytes{pod=~"comdemo.*"}) by (pod) would collect only memory utilized by the comdemo Emerald pods

Prometheus is just one very common way to collect system metrics in the modern application landscape. DevOps Test supports many more types of metrics collection across both modern and legacy systems, application types and databases that we don't cover here.

When we've selected the metrics we want to collect, click 'Save' and open the active resource monitor source. By expanding the Prometheus source in the table, we see that data is being collected and charted via a preview of the kubernetes metrics we can expect to see in our test reports later.
Before leaving the resource monitor config, give it a label "Emerald Metrics" which will allow us to identify associate the correct set of metrics when we run our test.

Our resource monitors are created and labelled, so we can move back to the test 'Execution' page and open the execution properties page for the "BenchmarkB2CFlow" test. Open the Resource Monitoring tab and select the label "Emerald Metrics" and click the 'Execute' button to start the performance schedule. Within a minute it should move from 'In Transition' to 'Running' in the Progress page, and once the test is running, you can open the Statistics Report in real-time. When the test is complete, look under 'Analyze...>Results' from the main left hand navigation and locate your Statistics Report in the right hand drawer.

The performance report is extremely comprehensive out of the box, and can be customised to suit your chosen metrics and dashboard styles. Explore the different tabs in the report to see different characteristics, and make sure to checkout the 'Resources' tab where our kuberenetes metrics from Emerald are collected! Obviously this test is only representative of a very small scale performance test, and in reality performance tests need to be run over much longer periods.

You might like to investigate DevOps Test Performance further by opening the scripts in the DevOps Test Performance desktop UI. However, test authoring is not covered in this tutorial, but please contact your HCL representative if you'd like to learn more about it!

We now revisit the staple of any test automation strategy, UI tests! Earlier we ran a UI automation suite to create new users in our Emerald application. Now those tests have run to completion, we can execute our second UI test suite 'Login Add Item and Checkout' which as the name suggests, logs into Emerald using the registered users, selects an item and flows thorugh a full checkout and order execution process. Find the test in our Execution view 'Execute...>Execution' and gesture to execute. Like before, we need to provide a label, and override values for hostname (your sandbox hostname) and port_num (port 443) in the variables tab before pressing Execute.

All of our tests should now have either run at least once, or be in the process of running and now we're ready to see what happens when a test fails! Back in our list of executable Test Suites, we want to re-run 'Register Users and Add Address'. Notice that the settings are sticky, and so we don't need to re-initialise things like the variables for hostname and port number. The first time this suite ran against the Emerald instance it creates our users, thereafter, unless we change the test data being used, it will fail since the users already exist
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure
Step 3 - Analysing And Correcting A Test Failure

Once our 'Register Users & Add Address' suite has completed, navigate to the Results view 'Analyze...>Results' and locate the latest execution result for our suite, it should indicate the verdict as a test failure. Click on the row representing the test failure which opens the right hand side drawer. There are several types of reports available including performance characteristics of the transactions invoked as well as raw console and logs. We will analyse the 'Functional Test Report' as it best illustrates what we wanted the test to achieve. Navigate down through the test report, expanding the details to reveal each step of the test. Note the green steps are the ones which succeeded, and the red ones where failures began. Clicking through each of the steps, we see a screenshot on the right (click to expand) and the step details in the center of the page. Click on the first red test step and note that there was a failure to locate a control on the page. Click the screenshot corresponding to the failed step and we note that there is a warning message in place of the confirmation page we were looking for; in essence, we've caused a test failure by trying to re-run our test suite and asking it to register the same set of users we registered previously. What we've got is a data issue, not a test failure!

Having the right data during testing is critical, sometimes we need specific static data, other times when we're working with stateful systems we might need a brand new data refresh, or even a complex set of inter-referenced data across a multiple target systems and databases. Our test 'Register Users and Add Address' was configured to execute using a specific, static dataset called 'Users.csv'.

In the Datasets view (Author...>Data..>) we see the Users.csv file, located in Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests/Emerald-UI-Tests/Emerald in the Datasets folder. Click on the Users.csv dataset to open it and see the rows of user data we used in our test. At this point we will explore 3 different strategies to 'fix' our failing test.

We could edit the Users dataset to modify our test users. Clicking on the pencil icon opens the dataset editor where we can make those changes. Honestly though, that approach isn't a good idea for this use case as we would constantly we editing data each time we want to re run our tests.

We could substitute a different dataset with different users. Expand Users_Set2 and notice there is a different set of users. We can substitute this dataset when we execute our test by clicking on the 'Data Sources' tab in the Execution dialog and selecting in from the dropdown 'Override' selector. Again though, this is not a particularly sane strategy for our example as we might then need to maintain many different datasets and know which ones we've used!

A better approach for our user registration is to synthetically generate user data each time we execute the test. Exactly for this purpose, we can use User.ddf file, which is located in Emerald-One-Test-Studio-Tests/Emerald-UI-Tests/Data in the 'Data definitions' directory, which represents our User schema

In years gone by, we might have taken nightly or weekly extracts of data from production systems. Perhaps, if we were concientious, we would have run crude data obfuscation routines to mitigate the use of PII, but more often data was scraped and used in test 'as is'. Today, with much more stringent data privacy laws like GDPR, we need to be hyper sensitive to the risks involved in test data!
