Perhaps, if we were concientious, we would have run crude data obfuscation routines to mitigate the use of PII, but more often data was scraped and used in test 'as is'. Today, with much more stringent data privacy laws like GDPR, we need to be hyper sensitive to the risks involved in test data!

User data definition file (User.ddf) is a very simple data dictionary modelling a 'User'. Our User is pre-configured to generate a random firstname, lastname, email address and phone number.
Open the Users data definition and click through the different dictionary fields to explore their configuration.

firstname and lastname are generated by randomly selecting from a provided sample names file containing thousands of examples
phone number is generated using a regular expression. There are regex templates provided and we can add our own to the lists
email address is generated using a data generator made available in the catalog, that you can display by selecting the 'Catalog' pane. It uses small function to concatenate the randomly selected firstname.lastname and appends a domain name
firstname and lastname are generated by randomly selecting from a provided sample names file containing thousands of examples
phone number is generated using a regular expression. There are regex templates provided and we can add our own to the lists

email address is generated using a data generator made available in the catalog, that you can display by selecting the 'Catalog' pane. It uses small function to concatenate the randomly selected firstname.lastname and appends a domain name

We can test and preview the generation of data here in the data definition editor by clicking the eye (Preview is the hover), it displayes generated data sample. Click 'Generate' and choose how many rows of data to fabricate before pressing 'Download'. The CSV we generated gets downloaded. There are also ways to emit datasets directly by clicking the '+' button in the navigator and selecting 'Dataset'. Note that ddf files (data definitions) can be used for overloading dataset when executing a test. It will generates fresh new data before and each time the test start.

Back in the Execution page, we can now open the Execution dialog for the "Register Users and Add Address" test suite. Selecting the "Data Sources" tab we see there are three options. "No override" meaning the test will execute using Users.csv, or "Users_Set2.csv" which will substitute for the second static dataset or the option to use our data definition "User.ddf". By selecting the User data definition, we could set the override options for number of rows, and if required, we can also set a seed value that enables us to retrieve the same test data again in the future if so required, though the main goal here is to generate fresh new data before each execution, but for now let's use the data definition file to generate a new dataset for our test execution.

Navigate to the "Author/Data" section, and select to create a new dataset by clicking on the + icon in the navigator. Creating an edit branch for that operation will lead you to the creation wizard. Choose "Emerald-UI-Tests" from the Application dropdown, and provide a name for the dataset, "Users3.csv" for example. Clicking on Next will take you to the next choice, where you'll be able to choose "Fabricate data" option, and select "User.ddf" in the next dropdown. Provide a number of rows, 5 for example, set the "Fabricate headers" selector to selected, and click "Create". The newly fabricated data opens and displays randomized entries.

Now you can go back to the "Execute/Execution" section and choose to launch "Register Users and Add Address" test by overriding the dataset to use "User3.csv" ("Data Source" tab). After the test finishes, with a PASS verdict, go to the Results where we can see in the Functional Test Report the details coming from "User3.csv".

We are finished running tests as part of this tutorial, but feel free to explore re-running, authoring new scripts and writing your own Tests by using the  test workbenches for DevOps Test Integrations and APIs, Performance and UI. You may also like to associate your Tests and Test Suites with different components of the System Model that we setup earlier. For example, we could associate tests related to the component 'Search' by clicking on the component and in the right hand drawer, adding Tests and Test Suites under the 'Associate' tab. Once we have associations, the most recent execution history and trends appear in the 'Overview' tab for that system model component - a handy at a glance way to see activity for any given part of our application topology. Additionally, when we look back into the results in 'Analyse...>Results' we see that the test results now show any associated components as a label, and the results become filterable by component for times when you're interested only in a subset of activity.

Before signing off, take a look at the 'Overview' page, where you'll find a summary of our tests and test suites, a breakdown of the different types of tests, most recent execution statuses, recent commits in our projects as well as showing any scheduled tests that are due to be executed in future.

At this point, we may consider going deeper into the test authoring experience with DevOps Test UI, API and Performance, but it is important first to put what we have seen into context. Very few people use DevOps Test Hub soley to trigger tests manually from the dashboard - far more likely, they will blend manual test execution with external services that trigger execution headlessly as part of a continuous test/deploy or DevOps pipeline. DevOps Test Hub integrates natively via plugins to many different CI/CD and DevOps platforms (DevOps Deploy, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, IBM ETM etc) and also exposes execution APIs and CLI to allow for any other toolchain integration.
Swagger documentation for the DevOps Test Hub Execution APIs is provided and can be accessed via the HCLSoftware Maketplace 'API Directory' category.
Executing a test via APIs is a three step process:

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.

Generate a new offline token from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard which will be used to identify the user calling the API. To create a new offline token, click the user icon in the top right hand corner of the DevOps Test Hub dashboard and select 'Create Token'
Pass your 'offline token' to the authentication service and retrieve a time sensitive 'access token'. Note that this token expires after approximately 300 seconds.
Make a request to the jobs service to execute a given test configuration, passing the access token, offline token and details of the test suite you want to run.
Make the authentication request in order to retrieve your temporary access token.
curl -X POST -H "Accept: application/json" -d refresh_token="offline token from OTS" https://devopstesthubhostname/rest/tokens/ -k
curl -X POST -H "Accept: application/json" -d refresh_token="offline token from OTS" https://devopstesthubhostname/rest/tokens/ -k
Copy the access token value from the response for use in the execution request below
Using the execution request template below, replace the highlighted values as below:

Replace the Bearer value with the access token you copied from the authentication request.
Replace the offlineToken with your own offline token. 
Replace the devopstesthubhostname with the hostname of your solution sandbox.
Replace the testAsset section with an appropriate configuration for your chosen test type and configurations. The easiest way to find the appropriate configuration is using the developer tools in your browser to capture the request made when executing a test from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard. Find the /execution? request and copy the JSON body from the request and use it to replace the one in the CURL sample given below. Typical values include:
-------- assetId is unique to your environment and can be found using the developer tools in your browser when executing a test
-------- tags takes the value you wish to use when labelling the test execution, use this to mimic an external tool execution ID
-------- environment name if required by API & Postman tests only
-------- project id is unique to your environment and can be found using the developer tools in your browser when executing a test
Replace the Bearer value with the access token you copied from the authentication request.
Replace the offlineToken with your own offline token.
Replace the devopstesthubhostname with the hostname of your solution sandbox.

Replace the testAsset section with an appropriate configuration for your chosen test type and configurations. The easiest way to find the appropriate configuration is using the developer tools in your browser to capture the request made when executing a test from the DevOps Test Hub dashboard. Find the /execution? request and copy the JSON body from the request and use it to replace the one in the CURL sample given below. Typical values include:

-------- tags takes the value you wish to use when labelling the test execution, use this to mimic an external tool execution ID
-------- environment name if required by API & Postman tests only
-------- project id is unique to your environment and can be found using the developer tools in your browser when executing a test

CURL template for execution:
Curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -H "Authorization: Bearer access token" -d 'testAsset' https://devopstesthubhostname/rest/projects/__projectid__/executions/

Example CURL command - executing an API test:
Curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCIgOiAiSldUIiwia2lkIiA6ICI0ejk5UmdBdDd4WHkwSGFhVmtzV2pDYlJ5WlByVmVMSXdERzYyZVhNWGFZIn0..V8QYU1CfxwAMNZfMuewXgk-nmaZFMCyHtSCTtvYcj_w5oxdM39Qg59i4dqc3-zf6u-87WW9eXZ8s5wtR1_Ae5kXQW02PvrJPKjJ9mAbHrYKElYCnCQrYot3jQQe27THG4ENTNquD7Oez9iyU9XM6fj13QSGcj09y6W9erUEDIAh7v6ALzBuuEeu2Dfr7-LIpd7WOmLD1JC-5MWLgwME7exU1bTXsWcWonuxMYptWF0IcOcVnMF2cbmnB-Y6zq_9zIaVQPtqYLXZyDFSeP0E5WovrGp4XCdkM3DTXtrjrVd0yi5F68SSQIAXvIzsH2kDyvPewapXAbk6XLC3tdrNbmg" -d '{"testAsset":{"assetId":"0211c4b0-974c-4c3a-91c4-b0974cdc3ad7","revision":"DEV","requestedVersion":null},"tags":["BuildID 000148"],"environment":"Commerce React Store PRIVATE","remoteLocations":[],"offlineToken":"eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCIgOiAiSldUIiwia2lkIiA6ICI3NmU1ZjkwYy1kMzM1LTRmMGItYmM1NC0yYzk3YWNhMzViZGYifQ..a3Pkn2Fy-T2KN-u3ScAPzrln55AZeTcLKgky-_v5va0"}' https://hcl-devopstesthub.sbx0045.play.sofy.dev/rest/projects/1250/executions/

The provided videos offer some guidance on the above tutorials. Note that there are small differences between the video and tutorial steps. There are also minor differences between the version of DevOps Test Hub used in the video and the one in your sandbox, users should refer to the written tutorial which is correct as at the current release and use the video for general guidance only.

On every node, resources will have been pre-allocated to essential kubernetes services, so nodes and clusters must be sized to account for that. For example, if a solution requires a minimum of 1 cpu on each node, the actual node size must be greater than 1 cpu to allow for some use by the installed kubernetes services.
